05 Planning in 3 Year Cycles - YouTube.mp4
At the core of the scalable planning system is the three-year target. Unlike the five to 10-year, big, hairy, audacious goals that some companies set, a three-year target is a crystal clear business objective that outlines what the company's top-line revenue, bottom-line profit, and total enterprise value will be in three years from the date the goal is set. And by the way, it's worth noting that since we don't follow an annual planning cadence, three-year targets can be set at any time during the year, meaning you don't have to wait for December or January to start this process. Now, as the name suggests, this goal only needs to be set once every three years, and it's typically determined at a one-day strategic planning offsite that we call the Clarity Day.
Now, I'm not going to cover the Clarity Day in this module because it's covered in much greater depth in the session. On building your clarity compass, but all you need to know for now is that once the three-year target is set, the rest of the scalable planning system can be put into motion. Using a tool called the 12Q planning grid, we can then break our three-year target into 12 quarters. Now, in case you haven't already figured it out, 12 quarters and three years—both representing the same amount of time—yet the former is far more digestible and executable than the latter. Okay, once our three-year plan has been broken into 12, 90-day chunks, it's time to shift from planning mode into execution mode.
